name: Luther Henderson
abstract: Luther Henderson (March 14, 1919 – July 29, 2003) was an American arranger, composer, orchestrator, and pianist best known for his contributions to Broadway musicals.

interviews.title: Luther Henderson — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

concerts.label: National Negro Opera Company: An Afternoon of Stars
A "Pops" Concert for the Young at Heart
My Favorite Broadway: The Leading Ladies
concerts.venue: Isaac Stern Auditorium / Ronald O. Perelman Stage

quotes.text: Okay. Now I had been a few years away from Lena Horne because I was with her in late '40s, maybe '50s, something like that. Anyway, the Symphony of the Air, Don Gillis had, I don't know, been commissioned that he was to do these works which he was already doing with his band. I don't know if he was doing Night Creature. I think Night Creature may have been done specifically for that concert. But at any rate, and he wanted to perform it at Carnegie Hall with a, in a symphony setting. And this is when he called me and I guess that would have been … it was done in 1955 so … I don't know, I didn't have a whole lot of time to do it, you know, because … but the point is that what I did was to follow him around the country wherever I could find … in other words, he didn't do like we do, you know, you sit down and okay we're gonna have a session, we're gonna write some music and so forth. No, no, you'd go and you'd follow him to whatever
quotes.caption: Luther Henderson recalls 1955 Symphony of the Air concert
quotes.doc_title: Luther Henderson — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Terry Clark
about_quotes.evidence: Luther Henderson and Dudley Brooks were two of our chief arrangers on staff at Great Lakes.
about_quotes.doc_title: Terry Clark — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Louie Bellson
about_quotes.evidence: Luther Henderson wrote the score out for Duke just on that one piece – Tone Parallel of Harlem... I took the score home and made a complete drum-set part and handed it to Duke on my way out, after we played with the symphony orchestra.
about_quotes.doc_title: Louie Bellson — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Eddie Bert
about_quotes.evidence: it was with a guy, I don't know if you know him, Luther Henderson. It was Luther Henderson, Leonard Ware on guitar, and a bass player, Slim. And everybody used to go in and jam.
about_quotes.doc_title: Eddie Bert interviewed by Monk Rowe, Danbury, Connecticut, November 20, 2001

about_quotes.speaker: Kenny Burrell
about_quotes.evidence: And so after that I, we were friends, kind of, you know. And so I would occasionally see him occasionally and got gradually to know his family...
about_quotes.doc_title: Kenny Burrell — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Jones Hank
about_quotes.evidence: Luther called me and said, 'Would you like to do this Broadway show?' ... I played on the stage with a little band and conducted from the piano.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jones Hank — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Tom Whaley
about_quotes.evidence: Luther Henderson, he says he saw me doing that ... and Luther said he saw me writing among all that turmoil and he said that was great.
